title: Interpreting the behavior of accumulation functions
description: Given the graph of a function, pick the appropriate justification for certain properties of an antiderivative of the function.
topic_path: Interpreting the behavior of accumulation functions involving area
Interpreting the behavior of accumulation functions

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of $f$.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis is unnumbered. The graph consists of a curve. The curve starts on the positive y-axis, moves upward concave up, and ends in quadrant 1.](web+graphie:${â˜£ LOCALPATH}/images/67077799e9810b29c07d4ad4a3e5400d8d7165cc)

Let $g(x)=\displaystyle\int_0^x f(t)\,dt$.

**What is an appropriate calculus-based justification for the fact that $g$ is increasing?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
Based on the second part of the fundamental theorem of calculus, $g$ is an antiderivative of $f$:

$g'=f$

This table summarizes the relationship between various properties of a function $f$ and its antiderivative $g$:

When the function $f$ is... | The antiderivative $g=\displaystyle\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ is...
:-: | :-:
Positive $+$ | Increasing $\nearrow$
Negative $-$ | Decreasing $\searrow$
Increasing $\nearrow$ | Concave up $\cup$
Decreasing $\searrow$ | Concave down $\cap$
Changes sign / crosses the $x$-axis | Extremum point
Extremum point | Inflection point

This is an appropriate calculus-based justification for the fact that $g$ is increasing:

$f$ is positive.

The curve starts on the negative y-axis, moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 4, moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 4, moves upward concave up to (c, 0), continues upward concave down to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 1, moves upward to a relative maximum in quadrant 1, moves downward to a relative minimum in quadrant 1, moves upward and ends in quadrant 1.](web+graphie:${â˜£ LOCALPATH}/images/74479f145a2f851abc59cb013eac459819b5f814)

Let $g(x)=\displaystyle\int_0^x f(t)\,dt$.

**What is an appropriate calculus-based justification for the fact that $g$ has a relative minimum at $x=c$?**

[[â˜ƒ radio 1]]
hints:
Based on the second part of the fundamental theorem of calculus, $g$ is an antiderivative of $f$:

$g'=f$

This table summarizes the relationship between various properties of a function $f$ and its antiderivative $g$:

When the function $f$ is... | The antiderivative $g=\displaystyle\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ is...
:-: | :-:
Positive $+$ | Increasing $\nearrow$
Negative $-$ | Decreasing $\searrow$
Increasing $\nearrow$ | Concave up $\cup$
Decreasing $\searrow$ | Concave down $\cap$
Changes sign / crosses the $x$-axis | Extremum point
Extremum point | Inflection point

This is an appropriate calculus-based justification for the fact that $g$ has a relative minimum at $x=c$:

$f$ crosses the $x$-axis from below it to above it.

choices.content: $f$ crosses the $x$-axis from below it to above it.
